title: Git Fundamentals
summary: Understand Git's three-tree model, branching strategies, merge vs rebase, and how teams collaborate without stepping on each other.
guide:
# Git Fundamentals

## What Even Is Git? (No Prior Knowledge Assumed)

Imagine you are writing a long essay and you save it as `essay.docx`.
Then you make changes and save again. Now you want to go back to yesterday's version —
but you only have one file. You are stuck.

Now imagine every time you save, you take a **snapshot** of the entire project,
label it with a message like 'added introduction', and can jump back to ANY
previous snapshot at any time. That is exactly what Git does.

Git is a **version control system** — software that tracks every change you make
to your code files, so you can:
- Go back to any previous version
- See exactly what changed and who changed it
- Work on new features without breaking the working version
- Collaborate with a team without overwriting each other's work

**Git vs GitHub — they are NOT the same thing:**
```
Git     = the tool on your computer that tracks changes
GitHub  = a website that hosts your Git repositories online
          (like Google Drive, but for code with Git superpowers)

Other hosting sites: GitLab, Bitbucket — all use Git underneath
```

---

## Installing Git and First-Time Setup

```bash
# Check if Git is already installed
git --version
# Output: git version 2.43.0

# Install on Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt install git

# Install on macOS
brew install git

# REQUIRED: tell Git who you are (stored in every commit)
git config --global user.name "Alice Smith"
git config --global user.email "alice@example.com"

# Set VS Code as default editor (optional but nice)
git config --global core.editor "code --wait"

# See all your config
git config --list
```

Why set name and email? Every commit you make gets stamped with this info.
Your teammates see 'Alice Smith committed: fixed login bug' — not just a random hash.

---

## The Three Areas — The Most Important Mental Model

Git manages your project through three distinct areas. Understanding this
eliminates 80% of Git confusion.

```
  WORKING TREE          STAGING AREA           REPOSITORY
  (your files)          (the index)            (.git folder)

  what you see          what you are           permanent
  and edit today        about to commit        history

  essay.txt             [essay.txt v2]         commit abc123
  style.css             [style.css v3]         commit def456
  app.js                                       commit ghi789

     |                       |                       |
     |------ git add ------->|                       |
     |                       |--- git commit ------->|
     |<------ git restore ---|                       |
     |<------ git checkout ---------------------------
```

**Analogy — the photographer:**
- **Working Tree** = the scene in front of you (messy, in progress)
- **Staging Area** = you are choosing which subjects to include in the photo
- **Repository** = the photo album (permanent snapshots, never deleted)

You stage only the changes relevant to one logical unit of work,
then commit them. This gives you a clean, meaningful history.

---

## Your First Repository — Step by Step

```bash
# OPTION A: Start fresh
mkdir my-project
cd my-project
git init
# Git creates a hidden .git/ folder — this IS the repository
# Output: Initialized empty Git repository in /my-project/.git/

# OPTION B: Download an existing project from GitHub
git clone https://github.com/someuser/some-repo.git
cd some-repo
# You now have a full copy with all history
```

---

## The Core Daily Workflow

This is what you will do dozens of times per day:

```bash
# STEP 1: Check what is going on
git status
# Shows:
# - Which files changed (red = unstaged, green = staged)
# - Which files are new (untracked)
# - Which branch you are on

# STEP 2: See the actual changes (the diff)
git diff             # unstaged changes (working tree vs staging area)
git diff --staged    # staged changes (staging area vs last commit)

# STEP 3: Stage the changes you want to commit
git add filename.txt    # stage one specific file
git add src/           # stage everything inside src/ folder
git add .              # stage EVERYTHING changed in this directory
git add -p             # interactive: stage specific chunks/lines (powerful!)

# STEP 4: Commit with a clear message
git commit -m "feat: add user login endpoint"
# The message is permanent — make it descriptive!

# STEP 5: Push to remote (GitHub/GitLab/etc.)
git push origin main
# origin = the name of the remote (GitHub)
# main   = the branch name

# GETTING latest changes from remote
git pull             # fetch + merge in one step
git fetch            # only download, do not merge yet
git pull --rebase    # fetch + rebase instead of merge (cleaner)
```

**What makes a good commit message?**
```
Bad:  'changes'  'fix'  'wip'  'asdf'
Good: 'feat: add password reset email flow'
      'fix: null pointer in UserService.findById()'
      'chore: upgrade spring-boot to 3.2.1'
      'docs: add API endpoint documentation'

Convention: <type>: <short summary>
Types: feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, style
```

---

## Branches — Working in Parallel

A branch is just a **lightweight pointer** to a specific commit.
Creating one is instant and costs almost nothing.

```
main branch:
  A --- B --- C          <- production code, always working
               \
feature/login:  D --- E  <- your new feature, in progress
                         <- nobody else is affected until you merge
```

```bash
git branch                    # list all branches (* = current)
git branch feature/login      # create a new branch (does NOT switch to it)
git checkout feature/login    # switch to a branch
git checkout -b feature/login # create AND switch in one command (shortcut)
git switch feature/login      # newer Git 2.23+ syntax for checkout
git switch -c feature/login   # create + switch (newer syntax)

# When feature is done: merge it back
git checkout main
git merge feature/login

# Clean up the branch after merging
git branch -d feature/login   # delete local branch
git push origin --delete feature/login  # delete remote branch
```

**Real-world scenario:** Three developers on one team:
```
Alice  creates: feature/payment-gateway
Bob    creates: feature/email-notifications
Charlie creates: fix/login-timeout-bug

All three branch FROM main.
Each works independently — no interference.
Each opens a Pull Request when done.
Code review happens. Approved? Merge to main.
Your CI/CD pipeline runs tests on every PR automatically.
```

---

## Merge vs Rebase — Explained Simply

Both combine changes from one branch into another. The difference is HOW.

**Merge** — joins two branches, creates a merge commit:
```
Before:
  main:    A --- B --- C
                \
  feature:  D --- E

After: git checkout main && git merge feature
  main:    A --- B --- C --- M   (M = merge commit)
                \           /
  feature:  D --- E ---------

History shows exactly WHEN branches met. Non-destructive.
```

**Rebase** — replays your commits on top of another branch:
```
Before:
  main:    A --- B --- C
                \
  feature:  D --- E

After: git checkout feature && git rebase main
  main:    A --- B --- C
                       \
  feature:              D' --- E'  (rewritten commits)

Linear history! D and E are rewritten with new hashes.
```

**When to use each:**
```
Merge:  merging feature branches into main (shows the full picture)
Rebase: updating your feature branch with latest main changes (local only)

GOLDEN RULE: Never rebase commits that others have already pulled.
Rebase rewrites history. If Bob has your old commits, his branch
will diverge and everything gets messy.
```

---

## Undoing Mistakes

Git's safety net — every mistake is recoverable (mostly).

```bash
# UNDO UNSTAGED CHANGES (working tree)
git restore filename.txt     # throw away changes to one file
git restore .                # throw away ALL unstaged changes (careful!)

# UNDO STAGED CHANGES (move back to working tree)
git restore --staged filename.txt  # unstage a file

# UNDO LAST COMMIT (but keep the changes)
git reset --soft HEAD~1
# HEAD~1 means 'one commit before current'
# Changes go back to staging area — you can re-commit differently

# UNDO LAST COMMIT AND DISCARD CHANGES (nuclear option)
git reset --hard HEAD~1
# WARNING: changes are gone from working tree too. No undo for this!

# SAFE UNDO for shared branches: create an 'anti-commit'
git revert abc1234
# Creates a NEW commit that reverses the changes of abc1234
# Safe because it does NOT rewrite history

# FIX YOUR LAST COMMIT (before pushing)
git commit --amend -m 'corrected message'
# Or: add a forgotten file, then amend:
git add forgotten-file.txt
git commit --amend --no-edit
```

```
Decision tree for undoing:

Did I push yet?
  NO:
    Unstaged change  --> git restore filename
    Staged change    --> git restore --staged filename
    Wrong commit msg --> git commit --amend
    Undo last commit --> git reset --soft HEAD~1
  YES (already pushed to shared branch):
    ALWAYS use git revert (never reset on shared branches!)
```

---

## Stashing — Saving Work in Progress

You are in the middle of a feature and your boss says 'urgent bug fix needed now!'.
You cannot commit half-done work. Stash saves it temporarily.

```bash
git stash             # save all uncommitted changes to a hidden stack
                      # working tree becomes clean again

git stash list        # see all saved stashes
# stash@{0}: WIP on feature/login: abc123 half-done auth logic

git stash pop         # restore the most recent stash and remove it
git stash apply       # restore without removing (keeps it in list)

git stash push -m 'half-done payment logic'  # stash with a label
git stash drop stash@{0}                      # delete a specific stash
```

---

## Viewing History

```bash
git log                        # full commit history
git log --oneline              # compact: one line per commit
git log --oneline --graph --all # ASCII graph of all branches
git log -5                     # last 5 commits only
git log --author 'Alice'       # commits by Alice
git log --since '2 weeks ago'  # commits from last 2 weeks
git log -- filename.txt        # commits that touched filename.txt

# See what changed in a specific commit
git show abc1234

# Who wrote each line of a file?
git blame src/UserService.java
# Output: each line prefixed with: hash (author date) line-number | code

# Find which commit introduced a bug (binary search!)
git bisect start
git bisect bad         # current commit is broken
git bisect good v1.0   # v1.0 was fine
# Git checks out the mid-point commit, you test it
git bisect good/bad    # tell Git the result, repeat
# Git narrows down to the exact breaking commit
git bisect reset       # exit bisect mode
```

---

## .gitignore — What NOT to Track

Some files should NEVER go into Git: build output, IDE configs, secrets.

```
# Create a .gitignore file in your project root:

# Java build output
target/
*.class
*.jar

# Secrets — CRITICAL: never commit these!
.env
application-secrets.yml
*.pem
credentials.json

# IDE files
.idea/
*.iml
.vscode/

# macOS
.DS_Store

# Node.js
node_modules/
```

**CRITICAL MISTAKE — Committed a Secret:**
```
Scenario: You committed an AWS API key in application.properties
Even if you delete it in the next commit, it is still in git history.
Anyone who clones the repo can find it with: git log -p

Solution:
1. Immediately rotate/revoke the key (make the leaked key useless)
2. Use BFG Repo Cleaner to remove it from history:
   java -jar bfg.jar --delete-files application.properties
3. Force-push the rewritten history
4. Add the file to .gitignore
```

---

## GitHub Flow — The Standard Team Workflow

```
1. Branch off main
   git checkout -b feature/my-feature

2. Commit your work
   (many small commits with clear messages)

3. Push to GitHub
   git push origin feature/my-feature

4. Open a Pull Request (PR) on GitHub
   - Describe what you did and why
   - Tag reviewers

5. Code review
   - Teammates comment on specific lines
   - You push fixes as new commits

6. CI/CD runs automated tests on your branch
   - Must pass before merge

7. Merge to main (squash-merge = one clean commit)

8. Deploy (auto-triggered from main)

9. Delete the feature branch
```

---

## Remote Repositories

```bash
git remote -v                         # see all remotes and their URLs
git remote add origin https://github.com/user/repo.git  # add a remote
git remote set-url origin <new-url>   # change remote URL

git push -u origin main               # push and set upstream (first time)
                                       # -u = --set-upstream
git push                               # after -u is set, just this
git push --force-with-lease            # force push safely (checks nobody else pushed)

# Work with forks (common in open source)
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/repo.git
git fetch upstream
git merge upstream/main               # sync your fork with original
```

---

## Detached HEAD — What It Means

```
Normally, HEAD points to a branch name:
  HEAD -> main -> commit abc123

Detached HEAD: HEAD points directly to a commit hash:
  HEAD -> commit abc123   (no branch!)

How you get here:
  git checkout abc1234   (checking out a specific old commit)
  git checkout v1.2.3    (checking out a tag)

What it means:
  You can look around and experiment.
  Any new commits you make are NOT connected to any branch.
  If you switch away, those commits become unreachable (lost!).

How to save work done in detached HEAD:
  git checkout -b my-experiment   <- creates a branch HERE
  Now your commits are safe on 'my-experiment' branch.
```

---

## Mind Map — Everything at a Glance

```
GIT FUNDAMENTALS
|
+-- THREE AREAS
|   +-- Working Tree (your files)
|   +-- Staging Area (git add)
|   +-- Repository   (git commit)
|
+-- CORE WORKFLOW
|   +-- git status    (what changed?)
|   +-- git add       (stage changes)
|   +-- git commit    (save snapshot)
|   +-- git push      (upload to remote)
|   +-- git pull      (download + merge)
|
+-- BRANCHES
|   +-- git checkout -b (create + switch)
|   +-- git merge       (join branches)
|   +-- git rebase      (linearize history)
|   +-- Pull Requests   (code review flow)
|
+-- UNDOING
|   +-- git restore      (discard changes)
|   +-- git reset --soft (undo commit, keep changes)
|   +-- git reset --hard (undo + discard)
|   +-- git revert       (safe undo for shared branches)
|   +-- git commit --amend (fix last commit)
|
+-- UTILITIES
|   +-- git stash         (save WIP temporarily)
|   +-- git log --graph   (visual history)
|   +-- git blame         (who wrote this line?)
|   +-- git bisect        (find breaking commit)
|   +-- .gitignore        (exclude files)
|
+-- REMOTES
    +-- origin            (your GitHub fork/repo)
    +-- upstream          (original repo)
    +-- git push/pull/fetch
```

---

## How Git Connects to Other Topics

- **CI/CD Pipelines**: Every git push triggers your pipeline. Branch strategy
  defines deployment flow. Merging to main = auto-deploy to production.
- **Docker**: Images are often tagged with git commit hashes (`image:abc1234`).
  Dockerfiles live in the git repo.
- **Linux**: You run all git commands from the bash terminal.
  Bash scripting skills help automate git workflows.
- **Code Review / PRs**: GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket are all built on top of Git.

---

## Common Beginner Mistakes

1. **Committing directly to main** — always branch. main should only receive merges.
2. **Vague commit messages** — 'fix stuff' is useless. Future-you will hate present-you.
3. **git add .** always — review what you stage. Use `git status` first.
4. **Committing .env or secrets** — add to .gitignore BEFORE first commit.
5. **git reset --hard on shared branches** — destroys history others have pulled.
   Use `git revert` instead.
6. **Not pulling before pushing** — leads to rejected pushes. Always `git pull` first.
7. **Rebase on shared branches** — rewrites history others depend on. Chaos ensues.

---

## References and Further Learning

### Videos (Watch These!)
- **Git and GitHub for Beginners - Crash Course** by freeCodeCamp:
  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RGOj5yH7evk
  - 1 hour. Covers everything from init to pull requests. Best starting point.
- **13 Advanced Git Tips and Tricks** by Fireship:
  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ecK3EnyGD8o
  - 8 minutes. Fast-paced demos of stash, bisect, reflog, and more.

### Free Books and Articles
- **Pro Git Book** (official, free, complete): https://git-scm.com/book
  - The definitive Git reference. Chapters 1-3 are essential for beginners.
- **Oh Shit, Git!** (common mistakes and how to fix them): https://ohshitgit.com/
  - Bookmark this. When you mess up (you will), this has the exact fix.

### Diagrams and Cheatsheets
- **Git Visual Cheatsheet**: https://ndpsoftware.com/git-cheatsheet.html
  - Interactive diagram showing how commands move changes between the three areas.
- **GitHub Git Cheatsheet** (PDF): https://education.github.com/git-cheat-sheet-education.pdf

### Practice
- **Learn Git Branching** (visual interactive): https://learngitbranching.js.org/
  - Best interactive Git tutorial. Visualizes branches as you type commands.
- **GitHub Skills**: https://skills.github.com/
  - Hands-on courses directly in GitHub repos.

questions.prompt: What is the Git index (staging area)?
choices: A list of all remote repositories
A snapshot of what will go into the next commit
The complete history of all commits
The remote copy of the repository
questions.explanation: The index (staging area) is a snapshot of what will go into your next commit. You explicitly add changes to it with 'git add', giving you precise control over what each commit contains.

questions.prompt: You committed a secret API key. What is the correct fix?
choices: Delete the file and recommit
Use git reset --soft to undo and recommit without the key
Use BFG Repo Cleaner or git filter-branch to rewrite history, then rotate the key
Add the file to .gitignore
questions.explanation: Deleting in a new commit leaves it in history. Reset only works if you haven't pushed. .gitignore doesn't remove already-tracked files. The correct approach is rewrite history AND rotate the key (treat it as compromised).

questions.prompt: What is 'detached HEAD' state?
choices: The repository has no commits
HEAD points directly to a commit rather than a branch
The remote branch is ahead of local
A merge conflict that hasn't been resolved
questions.explanation: Detached HEAD means HEAD points to a specific commit hash, not a branch. Commits made in this state have no branch pointing to them and will be garbage-collected unless you create a branch.

questions.prompt: When should you use `git revert` instead of `git reset`?
choices: When you want to rewrite history on a local branch
When the commits have already been pushed to a shared branch
When you want to delete all changes permanently
When working alone on a private branch
questions.explanation: git revert creates a new commit that undoes changes — safe for shared branches because it doesn't rewrite history. git reset rewrites history, causing problems for others who have the original commits.

questions.prompt: What does this series of commands result in?
questions.code:
git checkout main
git checkout -b feature/login
git commit -m 'add login'
git checkout main
git merge feature/login
choices: An error — you can't merge into main
feature/login commits are merged into main, main is now ahead
main is rebased on top of feature/login
No change — merge requires a PR
questions.explanation: This is a standard feature branch workflow. After committing on feature/login and merging into main, main now includes the 'add login' commit. This is a fast-forward merge if main had no new commits.

questions.prompt: To temporarily save your uncommitted changes without committing them (e.g. to switch branches quickly), you use: `git ___`
questions.explanation: git stash saves your working tree and index to a stack. git stash pop restores them. Useful when you need to switch context quickly.

questions.prompt: Which commands are safe to run on a shared branch that others are working on? (Select all that apply)
choices: git revert <hash>
git reset --hard HEAD~3
git commit -m 'fix'
git rebase main
questions.explanation: git revert is safe (adds new commit). git commit is safe (adds new commit). git reset --hard rewrites history (dangerous on shared branch). git rebase rewrites commit hashes (dangerous on shared branch).

questions.prompt: Order these steps for the GitHub Flow branching strategy:
items: Create a feature branch from main
Make commits on the feature branch
questions.explanation: GitHub Flow: branch from main → commit → open PR → review + merge → deploy. main is always deployable.

questions.prompt: Match each command to its effect:
right: Applies a specific commit from another branch
Binary search to find bug-introducing commit
Shows who last modified each line
questions.explanation: stash=save work temporarily, cherry-pick=apply specific commit, bisect=binary search history, blame=line-by-line authorship.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between `git fetch` and `git pull`?
choices: fetch downloads changes but doesn't merge; pull downloads AND merges
fetch is for branches; pull is for tags
pull is fetch + rebase; fetch is pull + merge
They are identical
questions.explanation: git fetch downloads remote changes to your local tracking branches but doesn't touch your working branch. git pull = git fetch + git merge (or rebase with --rebase flag). Use fetch when you want to inspect changes before merging.

questions.prompt: What does `git log --oneline --graph --all` display?
choices: Only commits on the current branch
A visual ASCII graph of all branches and their commit history
The diff of all commits
A list of all remote branches
questions.explanation: --graph adds ASCII art branch diagram, --all shows all branches (not just current), --oneline condenses each commit to one line. Together they give a bird's-eye view of branch topology.

questions.prompt: To unstage a file that you added with `git add` (without discarding your changes), run: `git restore ___ file.txt`
questions.explanation: git restore --staged file.txt removes the file from the index (staging area) but keeps your working tree changes intact.

questions.prompt: What does `git commit --amend` do?
choices: Creates a new commit on top of the last
Rewrites the last commit (message and/or content)
Reverts the last commit
Merges the last two commits
questions.explanation: git commit --amend replaces the last commit with a new one (same content + staged changes, or just a new message). Only use before pushing — amending changes the commit hash.

questions.prompt: In Git Flow, which branch do hotfixes branch from?
choices: develop
release
main
feature
questions.explanation: In Git Flow, hotfixes (urgent production bugs) branch from main (production) and merge back into both main AND develop. This ensures the fix is in production quickly and isn't lost from the development line.

questions.prompt: Which files/patterns should typically be in .gitignore? (Select all that apply)
choices: target/ (Maven build output)
src/main/java/ (source code)
.env (environment variables)
.idea/ (IntelliJ project files)
README.md
questions.explanation: Build artifacts (target/), secrets (.env), and IDE files (.idea/) should be gitignored. Source code (src/) and docs (README.md) should be committed.

questions.prompt: What is the golden rule of rebasing?
choices: Always rebase instead of merge
Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch
Rebase only on main, never on feature branches
Always rebase before opening a PR
questions.explanation: Rebasing rewrites commit hashes. If others have your original commits, their branches now diverge from the rewritten history, causing conflicts and confusion.

questions.prompt: What happens after running this command?
choices: The last commit is undone but changes remain staged
The last commit and its changes are permanently discarded from working tree
A new revert commit is created
The branch is deleted
questions.explanation: --hard moves HEAD back one commit AND resets the working tree and index to match. The changes from the removed commit are lost. Use git revert instead for shared branches.

questions.prompt: To find which commit introduced a specific bug using binary search, you use: `git ___`
questions.explanation: git bisect does a binary search through commit history. You mark commits as good/bad and Git narrows down to the exact commit that introduced the bug.

questions.prompt: What is a 'fast-forward merge'?
choices: A merge that skips the review process
A merge where the target branch pointer simply moves to the latest commit of the source branch (no new merge commit needed)
A merge that automatically resolves conflicts
A merge from a hotfix branch directly to production
questions.explanation: A fast-forward merge happens when the target branch (e.g. main) has no new commits since the feature branch diverged. Git just moves the main pointer forward — no merge commit needed, history stays linear.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are differences between `git merge` and `git rebase`? (Select all that apply)
choices: Merge preserves the original commit history; rebase rewrites it
Merge creates a merge commit; rebase (generally) does not
Rebase is always safer than merge
Rebase produces a linear history; merge may produce a non-linear one
questions.explanation: Merge preserves history (including branch points) and adds a merge commit. Rebase rewrites commits to create a linear history but changes commit hashes. Neither is 'always safer' — it depends on context.

flashcards.back: 1. Working Tree (your files), 2. Index/Staging (what will be committed), 3. Repository (committed snapshots). git add moves 1→2, git commit moves 2→3.
Merge preserves history + adds a merge commit. Rebase replays commits onto another branch for linear history (rewrites hashes).

flashcards.front: When is `git revert` safer than `git reset`?
flashcards.back: On shared branches. revert adds a new undo-commit (safe). reset rewrites history (breaks others' branches).
HEAD points to a commit hash, not a branch. Commits are unreachable after switching away. Fix: git checkout -b new-branch.

flashcards.front: How do you apply a single commit from another branch?
flashcards.back: git cherry-pick <commit-hash> — copies that commit's changes and creates a new commit on your branch.

flashcards.front: What files should always be in .gitignore?
flashcards.back: Build artifacts (target/, dist/), secrets (.env, *.pem), IDE files (.idea/, .vscode/), OS files (.DS_Store). Never commit secrets.

flashcards.front: What is GitHub Flow in 4 steps?
flashcards.back: 1. Branch from main, 2. Commit work, 3. Open PR, 4. Merge + deploy. Main is always production-ready.

flashcards.front: Difference between git fetch and git pull?
flashcards.back: fetch = download remote changes only. pull = fetch + merge. Use fetch to inspect before merging.

project.brief:
## Project: Design a Branching Strategy for a 3-Person Startup

You're one of three engineers at a startup. Your team ships to production 2–3 times per week. You have:
- A `main` branch (production)
- Occasional hotfixes
- Regular feature work
- No dedicated QA team — you rely on PR reviews

**Your task:** Design a complete branching strategy. Cover:
1. Which branches exist and their purpose
2. How features are developed and merged
3. How hotfixes are handled
4. What goes in .gitignore
5. Your commit message convention (e.g. Conventional Commits: feat:, fix:, chore:)

Explain your choices. Why is this better than Git Flow for a 3-person startup?

checklist.text: Defined a clear branch hierarchy (e.g. main, feature/*, hotfix/*) with purpose of each
Described the feature development lifecycle: branch → commit → PR → review → merge
Handled hotfixes correctly: branch from main, merge back to main AND develop/main
Specified what goes in .gitignore (secrets, build artifacts, IDE files)
Proposed a commit message convention and explained why it helps
Justified why simpler flow (e.g. GitHub Flow) is better than full Git Flow for a small team

hints: A 3-person startup doesn't need Git Flow's complexity. GitHub Flow (branch from main, PR, merge, deploy) is usually enough. Ask yourself: what's the cost of each branch you create?
For hotfixes: the key constraint is they must go to production FAST and must also appear in the ongoing development line. How do you merge to both?
Conventional Commits (feat:, fix:, docs:, chore:) let you auto-generate changelogs and control semantic versioning. Reference: conventionalcommits.org
